attrs.alt: The DOM Tree -- HTML source is parsed into a tree of node objects; node types include Element, Text, Comment, and Document

content.text: (DOM) is the browser's live, in-memory representation of an HTML (or XML) document as a tree of
. When the browser parses HTML it builds this tree; JavaScript can read and modify it, and any change is immediately reflected in the rendered page.

part of JavaScript -- it is a platform API defined by the WHATWG DOM Standard that browsers expose to scripting languages. The same tree is accessible to JavaScript, WebAssembly, and browser extensions alike.
Every piece of an HTML document maps to a node in the tree:

Every element class inherits from a chain of interfaces that progressively add capabilities. Understanding this hierarchy explains which properties and methods are available on any given element.
. This limits the search to that element's subtree and is more efficient on large pages.
lets you react to DOM changes asynchronously. It is more efficient than polling and replaces the deprecated

MutationObserver callbacks fire asynchronously (microtask queue, after the current task), so multiple rapid DOM mutations are batched into one callback. This is more efficient than synchronous event listeners for tracking structural changes.
notifies you when an element's size changes, without needing to listen to the
event. It fires for content-box or border-box changes regardless of what caused the resize.

lets an element host a separate, encapsulated DOM tree. Styles inside the shadow tree do not leak out, and external styles do not leak in (by default). Angular uses shadow DOM optionally through ViewEncapsulation.

(drawing pixels). DOM operations that change geometry trigger reflow; those that change only colour or opacity trigger only repaint. Composite-only changes (
Batch writes in DocumentFragment then append once
Chrome/Firefox DevTools expose the DOM interactively. Understanding the tools accelerates debugging.
Elements panel: inspect and live-edit the DOM tree and CSS; hover highlights the element on-page
Console: $0 is the last selected element in the Elements panel; $('selector') is a shortcut for document.querySelector
$$('selector') in the console returns an array from querySelectorAll (Chrome-only shortcut)
Right-click an element > Break on > Subtree modifications -- pauses JavaScript when the DOM under that element changes (great for debugging mystery DOM mutations)
Accessibility tree (Accessibility tab) shows the ARIA roles and labels Angular binds -- verify [attr.aria-*] bindings here

// Console shortcuts (Chrome DevTools only)
$('button.submit')        // equivalent to document.querySelector
$$('input[type="text"]')  // equivalent to Array.from(document.querySelectorAll(...))
$0                        // last element selected in Elements panel
getEventListeners($0)     // lists all event listeners on the selected element (DevTools only)

to guard any code that touches browser-only APIs. Inject

built alongside the visual tree. Screen readers and assistive technologies navigate this tree, not the visual DOM. ARIA attributes modify how elements are represented.
The DOM is the browser's structured, live representation of an HTML document. Key points:
The DOM is a platform API -- not part of JavaScript, but exposed to it by browsers
Every HTML construct maps to a node: elements, text, comments, the document root
Node is the base interface; Element extends it; HTMLElement and specific elements extend further
document is the global root -- the entry point for all queries and node creation
getElementsBy* returns live collections; querySelectorAll returns a static NodeList
DOMContentLoaded fires when HTML is parsed; window load fires when all resources are ready
Angular compiles templates into DOM instructions; use Renderer2 or template bindings instead of direct nativeElement access
